title: Global Issues in Medicine
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Joseph J. Rhatigan, Paul E. Farmer

items.subtopic: Global Issues in Medicine | WHY GLOBAL HEALTH?
items.title: Importance of Global Health Equity
items.content: Global health aims to improve the health of all people worldwide and achieve health equity by addressing transnational problems. This field has gained momentum due to increased investments aimed at tackling health issues faced by impoverished populations.
keyPoints: Focus on improving global health and achieving health equity
Addressing transnational health problems
Increased investment in health problems of poor populations
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · Global Issues in Medicine | WHY GLOBAL HEALTH?

items.subtopic: Global Issues in Medicine | WHY GLOBAL HEALTH?
items.title: Barriers to Global Health Improvement
items.content: The chapter identifies significant barriers to improving the health of people without access to modern medicine, including issues related to HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, malaria, Ebola, COVID-19, and noncommunicable chronic diseases. It emphasizes the need for strengthening health systems and improving healthcare delivery.
keyPoints: Barriers include lack of access to modern medicine
Focus on infectious diseases like HIV/AIDS, TB, malaria, Ebola, COVID-19
Noncommunicable diseases are also significant barriers
Need to strengthen health systems and improve care delivery
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · Global Issues in Medicine | WHY GLOBAL HEALTH?

items.subtopic: A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 1)
items.title: The Role of WHO in Global Health Successes
items.content: The World Health Organization (WHO) played a pivotal role in the eradication of smallpox, achieving success in 1979 despite geopolitical tensions.
keyPoints: WHO was formed in 1948 as the first truly global health institution.
Smallpox eradication campaign succeeded in 1979 under WHO's leadership.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 1)
items.title: The Alma-Ata Declaration and GOBI Strategy
items.content: The Alma-Ata Conference in 1978 set a goal for 'Health for All by the Year 2000' through universal primary health care, leading to the development of the GOBI strategy.
keyPoints: Alma-Ata Conference in 1978 aimed for 'Health for All by the Year 2000'.
GOBI included growth monitoring, oral rehydration, breast-feeding, and immunizations.
Expanded to GOBI-FFF with female education, food, and family planning.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 1)
items.title: Impact of Structural Adjustment Policies on Global Health
items.content: Structural adjustment policies in the 1980s led to reduced public health spending, contributing to a resurgence of diseases and high maternal mortality rates.
keyPoints: Structural adjustments encouraged reduced public expenditures in health.
Resurgence of diseases like malaria and tuberculosis occurred.
500,000 women died annually from childbirth-related causes in the 1990s.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 2)
items.title: Impact of HIV/AIDS on Global Health Initiatives
items.content: The emergence of HIV/AIDS in the early 1980s marked a significant turning point, highlighting the persistent threat of infectious diseases and prompting new global health strategies. This led to the establishment of key multilateral institutions like the Global Fund and bilateral efforts such as PEPFAR.
keyPoints: HIV/AIDS was first described in medical literature in 1981.
By the early 21st century, HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria together killed nearly 6 million people annually.
The rise of institutions like the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria; PEPFAR; and the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation marked a shift in global health financing.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 2)
items.title: Challenges and Goals in Global Health Governance
items.content: Despite advancements, coordination among global health institutions remains problematic. The COVID-19 pandemic exposed these flaws, emphasizing the need for effective governance structures to address health equity and strengthen national health systems.
keyPoints: The WHO's International Health Regulations were strengthened in May 2007 following SARS in 2003.
Global response to COVID-19 highlighted both strengths and fundamental flaws in global health governance.

Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted in 2015 include Goal 3, which aims for universal health coverage by 2030 with targets like reducing maternal and child mortality and ending HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria epidemics.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · A BRIEF HISTORY OF GLOBAL HEALTH INSTITUTIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ECONOMICS OF GLOBAL HEALTH | MORTALITY AND THE GLOBAL BURDEN OF DISEASE
items.title: Investment in Global Health and Economic Development
items.content: Global health interventions are often influenced by economic considerations, with a consensus that investments in healthcare delivery and communicable disease control can boost productivity. The debate centers on whether economic development or addressing ill health should be prioritized to initiate this cycle.
keyPoints: Investments in healthcare lead to increased productivity.
Health aid grew from $5.6 billion in 1990 to $38.9 billion in 2018, reaching $64.7 billion in 2021 due to COVID-19 response.
Leading contributors include the United States, the United Kingdom, Germany, and private foundations.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · THE ECONOMICS OF GLOBAL HEALTH | MORTALITY AND THE GLOBAL BURDEN OF DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE ECONOMICS OF GLOBAL HEALTH | MORTALITY AND THE GLOBAL BURDEN OF DISEASE
items.title: Understanding Global Burden of Disease through DALYs
items.content: The concept of Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) is crucial for assessing the global burden of disease, combining years lost due to premature death and disability. This metric has been instrumental in understanding health status in developing countries.
keyPoints: DALY combines years of life lost and years lived with disability.
Introduced by the World Bank's 1993 World Development Report Investing in Health.
Used for cost-effectiveness analysis of health interventions.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · THE ECONOMICS OF GLOBAL HEALTH | MORTALITY AND THE GLOBAL BURDEN OF DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE ECONOMICS OF GLOBAL HEALTH | MORTALITY AND THE GLOBAL BURDEN OF DISEASE
items.title: Global Burden of Diseases, Injuries, and Risk Factors (GBD) Study
items.content: The GBD study provides comprehensive estimates of disease burden globally, using improved data collection and analysis methods. It includes detailed reports on 386 diseases and injuries, highlighting trends from 1990 to 2019.
keyPoints: GBD is the most comprehensive effort for global disease burden estimation.
Reports released in 2013, 2015, 2017, and 2019 with updates on health-state severity weight system.
Examines trends from 1990 to 2019 across major diseases, injuries, and risk factors.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · THE ECONOMICS OF GLOBAL HEALTH | MORTALITY AND THE GLOBAL BURDEN OF DISEASE

items.title: Shift from Communicable to Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in Global Mortality
items.content: The burden of global mortality has shifted significantly over the past few decades, with a marked decrease in deaths due to communicable diseases, maternal and neonatal conditions, and nutritional deficiencies. In contrast, there has been a dramatic rise in deaths from non-communicable diseases (NCDs), such as ischemic heart disease and stroke.
keyPoints: In 2019, 17% of global deaths were due to communicable diseases, maternal and neonatal conditions, and nutritional deficiencies.
Ischemic heart disease was the leading cause of death worldwide in 2019, accounting for 16% of total deaths.
Stroke was the second leading cause of death globally, causing 11% of global mortality.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · GLOBAL MORTALITY

items.title: Regional Disparities in Causes of Death
items.content: There are significant regional disparities in the causes of death. In Sub-Saharan Africa and southern Asia, a larger proportion of deaths are due to communicable diseases compared to high-income countries where NCDs predominate.
keyPoints: 78% of deaths from communicable diseases, maternal and neonatal conditions, and nutritional deficiencies occurred in Sub-Saharan Africa and southern Asia.
Ischemic heart disease accounted for only 5% of total deaths in Sub-Saharan Africa.
Among the top 10 causes of death in Sub-Saharan Africa, five were infectious diseases.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · GLOBAL MORTALITY

items.title: Progress and Challenges in Reducing Childhood Mortality
items.content: Significant progress has been made in reducing childhood mortality globally, with a notable decrease in deaths among children under 5 years. However, the majority of these deaths still occur in Sub-Saharan Africa and southern Asia.
keyPoints: Deaths among children under 5 years dropped from 16.4 million in 1970 to 5.0 million in 2019.
46% of childhood deaths in 2021 occurred in the neonatal period.
Slightly more than one-half of deaths among children under 5 years old occurred in Sub-Saharan Africa.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · GLOBAL MORTALITY

items.subtopic: HEALTH AND WEALTH | RISK FACTORS FOR DISEASE BURDEN
items.title: Link Between Poverty and Health Outcomes
items.content: Poverty is a significant determinant of health, with extreme poverty affecting 9% of the global population in 2023. The COVID-19 pandemic exacerbated poverty levels, particularly impacting low- and middle-income countries.
keyPoints: 9% (700 million) lived on less than $2.15 per day as a measure of extreme poverty.
50% of those living in extreme poverty are located in Sub-Saharan Africa.
Children make up 31% of the global population but account for 50% of those in poverty.
COVID-19 pandemic increased poverty, with the poorest losing twice as much income as the richest between 2020 and 2022.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · HEALTH AND WEALTH | RISK FACTORS FOR DISEASE BURDEN

items.subtopic: HEALTH AND WEALTH | RISK FACTORS FOR DISEASE BURDEN
items.title: Leading Risk Factors for Global Disease Burden
items.content: The leading risk factors for global disease burden in 2019 were high systolic blood pressure, smoking, and high fasting plasma glucose. Despite a decline, maternal and childhood malnutrition remain critical for child mortality under five.
keyPoints: Top three risk factors for global disease burden in 2019: high systolic blood pressure, smoking, high fasting plasma glucose.
Maternal and childhood malnutrition are leading causes of death among children <5 years.
24% of all deaths and 28% of deaths in children <5 years were due to modifiable environmental factors.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · HEALTH AND WEALTH | RISK FACTORS FOR DISEASE BURDEN

items.subtopic: HEALTH AND WEALTH | RISK FACTORS FOR DISEASE BURDEN
items.title: Environmental Factors and Disease Burden
items.content: Modifiable environmental factors contribute significantly to global mortality, particularly among children under five. Indoor air pollution from solid fuels is a major contributor to lower respiratory infections.
keyPoints: 24% of all deaths and 28% of child <5 years deaths in 2019 were due to modifiable environmental factors.
17 million children die annually from causes related to unhealthy environments, including lack of clean water and sanitation.
Indoor air pollution accounts for 25% of DALYs from lower respiratory infections.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · HEALTH AND WEALTH | RISK FACTORS FOR DISEASE BURDEN

items.title: Impact of ART on HIV Management in High-Income vs. Developing Countries
items.content: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) has transformed HIV from a fatal disease to a manageable chronic condition, especially in high-income countries since the mid-1990s. In contrast, widespread access to ART in developing countries began only after 2003 due to initial barriers such as cost and infrastructure limitations.
keyPoints: In the United States, ART has significantly increased life expectancy for people with HIV, approaching that of the general population.
Before 2003, arguments against rapid ART implementation included high costs, complexity, lack of monitoring infrastructure, and insufficient trained healthcare providers.
By 2022, 76% of people living with HIV in low- and middle-income countries had access to ART.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · HIV INFECTION/AIDS (part 1)

items.title: Achieving UNAIDS' 90-90-90 and 95-95-95 Targets
items.content: The ambitious UNAIDS targets aimed for significant improvements in HIV treatment coverage and outcomes. By 2023, five countries achieved the updated '95-95-95' goals ahead of schedule.
keyPoints: UNAIDS set the 90-90-90 targets: 90% knowing their status, 90% treated with ART, and 90% achieving viral load suppression by 2020.
Five countries met these goals by 2020: Botswana, Eswatini, Rwanda, United Republic of Tanzania, and Zimbabwe.
The '95-95-95' targets for 2030 were achieved by the same five countries by 2023.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · HIV INFECTION/AIDS (part 1)

items.title: Impact of ART Accessibility on HIV Transmission Rates
items.content: The widespread availability and affordability of antiretroviral therapy (ART) have significantly reduced HIV transmission rates. The introduction of generic ART medications, supported by initiatives like the Clinton Health Access Initiative and Médecins Sans Frontières, has made first-line regimens available for less than $45 per year in low- and middle-income countries.
keyPoints: First-line ART cost dropped from >$10,000 to <$45 per year due to generic medications.
WHO's public health approach reduced transmission rates significantly.
A 96% decline in HIV transmission was observed with the rollout of ART in Africa and the Caribbean.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · HIV INFECTION/AIDS (part 2)

items.title: Rwanda's Success in Reducing HIV Mortality
items.content: Rwanda exemplifies how strong national leadership, evidence-based policy, and a health-systems approach can lead to substantial reductions in HIV mortality. Despite its low gross national income, Rwanda achieved almost universal access to ART, resulting in an 85% decrease in HIV-related deaths since 2000.
keyPoints: Rwanda's HIV mortality decreased by 85% due to near-universal ART access.
Key factors include strong leadership, cross-sector collaboration, and community-based care.
The health-systems approach integrates HIV treatment into primary healthcare.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · HIV INFECTION/AIDS (part 2)

items.title: Global Impact and HIV Co-infection
items.content: Tuberculosis (TB) remains a leading cause of death globally, second only to COVID-19 in adults. The disease's burden is exacerbated by its close link with HIV infection, particularly in regions like southern Africa.
keyPoints: In 2022, TB caused an estimated 1.3 million deaths worldwide.
890,000 new TB cases occurred among people living with HIV out of 10.6 million total new cases.
HIV co-infection significantly contributes to the resurgence of TB in southern Africa.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS

items.title: Limitations of DOTS Strategy
items.content: The DOTS strategy, while effective for drug-susceptible TB, has limitations including low sensitivity of sputum smear microscopy and challenges in passive case-finding.
keyPoints: Sputum smear microscopy misses many pulmonary and all extrapulmonary TB cases.
Passive case-finding depends on uneven healthcare access.
MDR-TB requires more than isoniazid and rifampin due to resistance.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS

items.title: Antibiotic Resistance Crisis
items.content: The rise of MDR- and XDR-TB strains poses a significant challenge, with poor infection control contributing to epidemics.
keyPoints: 4% of new TB cases in 2021 were rifampin-resistant or MDR.
18% of previously treated patients had MDR-TB.
450,000 estimated MDR-TB cases occurred in 2021, with 8% being XDR.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS

items.subtopic: TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 1)
items.title: Multidrug Regimens for Chronic Infections
items.content: Chronic infections, including tuberculosis and drug-resistant HIV, are effectively managed with multidrug regimens tailored to the susceptibility of infecting strains. This approach is applicable across various pathogens such as bacteria, fungi, parasites, or viruses.
keyPoints: Multidrug regimens are essential for treating chronic infections due to various pathogens.
Even acute infections like those caused by Plasmodium species require multiple drugs for reliable treatment.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 1)

items.subtopic: TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 1)
items.title: Economic Barriers to AIDS Prevention and Care
items.content: Charging fees for AIDS prevention and care is problematic for impoverished populations, highlighting the need for these services to be considered public goods. Subsidies and increased local investments can reduce costs significantly.
keyPoints: AIDS prevention and care should be seen as a public good.
ART can cost less than $0.13 per day with generic medications.
Increased national health investments, like in Rwanda, can make programs sustainable.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 1)

items.subtopic: TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 1)
items.title: Healthcare Personnel Shortages and Brain Drain
items.content: A significant shortage of healthcare personnel, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa, exacerbates health challenges. The WHO recommends a minimum of 1 physician per 1000 persons, but many regions fall short, with rural areas being disproportionately affected.
keyPoints: WHO recommends 1 physician per 1000 people; ~45% of member states do not meet this target.
Sub-Saharan Africa has 0.3 physicians per 1000 people.
~50% of WHO member states have fewer than 3 nurses and midwives per 1000 population.
75% of Sierra Leone's health workforce is in urban areas, where only 44% of the population resides.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 1)

items.subtopic: TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 2) | MALARIA
items.title: Addressing Barriers to Healthcare in Chronic Diseases
items.content: Extreme poverty presents significant barriers to healthcare adherence for chronic diseases. These can be mitigated through 'wrap-around services' such as food supplements, transportation assistance, childcare, and housing support.
keyPoints: Wrap-around services are essential to overcome barriers posed by extreme poverty in managing chronic diseases.
Poverty is the greatest obstacle to scaling up treatment and prevention services for chronic diseases.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 2) | MALARIA

items.subtopic: TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 2) | MALARIA
items.title: Economic Impact of Malaria on Endemic Regions
items.content: Malaria significantly impacts the economies of endemic countries, reducing per capita gross national product by up to 50% compared to non-endemic regions. This economic burden is due to factors like impaired cognitive development and decreased foreign investment.
keyPoints: Malaria may reduce per capita GNP by 50% in endemic countries.
Direct and indirect costs of malaria can consume over 10% of a household's annual income in endemic areas.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 2) | MALARIA

items.subtopic: TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 2) | MALARIA
items.title: Strategies for Effective Malaria Control
items.content: The Global Malaria Action Plan integrates prevention and care with strategies like insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, and artemisinin-based combination therapy. These efforts have reduced malaria cases by 1.7 billion and saved 10.6 million lives.
keyPoints: In 2022, there were 249 million malaria cases and 608,000 deaths globally.
Sub-Saharan Africa accounted for about 94% of all malaria cases and 95% of deaths.
Strategies include insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, and artemisinin-based combination therapy.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · TUBERCULOSIS AND AIDS AS CHRONIC DISEASES: LESSONS LEARNED (part 2) | MALARIA

items.title: Understanding the Impact of Health System Weakness on Ebola Outbreaks
items.content: The 2013–2016 West African Ebola epidemic was exacerbated by weak health systems, characterized by insufficient healthcare infrastructure and resources. This led to high transmission rates due to inadequate infection control and unsafe burial practices.
keyPoints: >28,000 recorded cases and 11,000 deaths during the 2013-2016 outbreak
One physician per 100,000 persons in Liberia before the epidemic
High maternal mortality ratio in Sierra Leone: 1180 deaths per 100,000 live births
WHO estimated case-fatality rate ~70% due to substandard care
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · EBOLA

items.title: Challenges and Strategies in Containing Ebola Outbreaks
items.content: Efforts to contain Ebola outbreaks often prioritize infection prevention over treatment. This approach, combined with mistrust and inadequate healthcare facilities, hampers effective epidemic response.
keyPoints: Preventive strategies prioritized over therapeutic care
High case-fatality rates due to substandard care
Mistrust and punitive measures deterred cooperation with health interventions
Epidemic of mistrust impeded surveillance and patient isolation
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · EBOLA

items.title: Advancements in Ebola Management: Lessons from the DRC Outbreak
items.content: The 2018-2020 Ebola outbreak in the DRC saw improved outcomes due to new tools like vaccines and enhanced supportive care, despite challenges posed by armed conflict.
keyPoints: ~3500 cases and two-thirds fatalities during the DRC outbreak
Ring vaccination strategy deployed with a new vaccine
Improved quality of supportive care: better staffing, monitoring, and clinical protocols
Case-fatality rate remained high, indicating unequal access to medical innovations
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · EBOLA

items.title: Impact of COVID-19 on Global Health Systems
items.content: The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted significant structural deficiencies in global health systems, particularly in recognizing and containing novel pathogens. High-income countries experienced strained healthcare systems due to the surge of patients, while low-income communities faced challenges with social distancing and quarantine.
keyPoints: COVID-19 was among the top five causes of death worldwide during its first four years.
Confirmed cases: 780 million; Confirmed deaths: over 7 million; Estimated total deaths: ~18 million.
High attack rates in vulnerable populations, such as homeless individuals and nursing home residents.
Underinvestment in public health systems for surveillance testing and contact tracing was evident in the U.S.
Global inequities in vaccine access were significant, with COVAX delivering 2 billion doses and averting an estimated 2.7 million deaths.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · COVID-19

items.title: Challenges in Global Vaccine Distribution
items.content: The COVID-19 pandemic exposed profound inequities in global access to vaccines and therapeutics, with high-income countries securing excess vaccine doses for themselves. The COVAX initiative aimed to address these disparities but faced challenges meeting its targets due to limited vaccine availability.
keyPoints: COVAX had broad support but failed to meet its distribution goals.
Excess procurement by high-income countries hindered global vaccine access.
COVAX delivered 2 billion doses, potentially averting 2.7 million deaths.
The pandemic underscored the need for universal healthcare access and stronger global public health systems.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · COVID-19

items.title: Global Burden of NCDs and the Double Disease Load
items.content: Noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) are responsible for a significant portion of global mortality, with 74% of all deaths worldwide in 2022 attributed to them. This burden is particularly heavy in low- and middle-income countries, where over three-quarters of NCD-related deaths occur, representing ~17 million early deaths annually. These regions face a 'double burden' of both communicable and noncommunicable diseases.
keyPoints: 74% of all global deaths in 2022 were due to NCDs.
~17 million early NCD-related deaths occur annually in low- and middle-income countries, accounting for 85% of such deaths worldwide.
By 2030, NCDs are predicted to cause 52 million deaths annually if no action is taken.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Diabetes Prevalence and Impact
items.content: The number of adults with diabetes is projected to rise significantly, from 537 million in 2022 to 783 million by 2045. A large proportion of these patients reside in developing countries, where younger individuals are more affected, leading to a greater impact on disability and mortality due to complications.
keyPoints: Diabetes affects ~1 in 11 adults globally as of 2022.
Projected increase to 783 million diabetic adults by 2045.
3 in 4 adults with diabetes live in developing countries, where the condition leads to significant disability and mortality.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Challenges of Cardiovascular Disease Management in Sub-Saharan Africa
items.content: In Sub-Saharan Africa, the impact of cardiovascular diseases is under-researched, with persistently elevated blood pressure often going undetected and untreated. This lack of management contributes to ongoing health challenges, despite modestly elevated blood pressure posing little immediate risk.
keyPoints: Systemic investigation into stroke and heart failure causes in Sub-Saharan Africa is recent.
Persistently elevated blood pressure remains largely undetected and untreated.
Rural health centers need access to antihypertensive medications.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Global Disparities in Heart Failure Causes and Management
items.content: Heart failure epidemiology varies globally, with high-income countries seeing more cases due to coronary artery disease and hypertension among the elderly. In contrast, low-income regions experience heart failure from poverty-related infections like HIV and untreated hypertension.
keyPoints: Heart failure accounts for ~3% of all hospitalizations in the US and Europe.
In Sub-Saharan Africa, nonischemic cardiomyopathies account for >90% of cardiac failures.
Peripartum cardiomyopathy incidence is 1 per 300 live births in rural Haiti.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease: A Persistent Global Health Challenge
items.content: RHD affects nearly 40 million people worldwide, with ~2.8 million new cases annually. It is a leading cause of heart failure in Sub-Saharan Africa and results in over 300,000 deaths per year, primarily in developing countries.
keyPoints: RHD leads to >300,000 deaths per year, mostly in developing nations.
A meta-analysis found RHD as the third most common cause of heart failure in Sub-Saharan Africa.
High prevalence of RHD among schoolchildren has been confirmed in Rwanda and Ethiopia.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Strategies for Eliminating Rheumatic Heart Disease
items.content: Elimination of RHD may rely on active case-finding and expanding surgical access. Partnerships can enhance regional centers' capacity to provide high-quality care, as seen in successful examples from Costa Rica, Cuba, and some Caribbean nations.
keyPoints: Active case-finding with echocardiography is crucial for high-risk groups.
Expanding surgical interventions is essential for children with advanced valvular damage.
Partnerships can develop regional centers of excellence to provide consistent care.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Cancer Burden in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
items.content: Low- and middle-income countries bear a significant burden of cancer mortality, accounting for approximately 70% of the 10 million global deaths in 2020. By 2040, annual cancer-related deaths are expected to exceed 16 million, with developing nations experiencing sharper increases due to lifestyle changes, sociocultural factors, genetics, and poverty.
keyPoints: Low- and middle-income countries accounted for ~70% of the 10 million global cancer deaths in 2020.
Projected annual cancer mortality by 2040 is expected to exceed >16 million deaths.
Cancer-causing infections like HPV, hepatitis B virus, and Helicobacter pylori contribute up to 50% of cases in some Sub-Saharan African countries.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 3)

items.title: Impact of Tobacco on Cancer Mortality
items.content: Tobacco use is a major contributor to cancer mortality, particularly in low- and middle-income countries where smoking rates are increasing. It is the leading cause of lung, bladder, and oral cancers, with tobacco-related diseases projected to cause over 1 billion deaths in the twenty-first century.
keyPoints: Tobacco use is a primary source of increased mortality from lung, bladder, and oral cancers.
>80% of the world's 1.1 billion smokers reside in low- and middle-income countries.
In 2019, tobacco use caused 8.7 million deaths globally.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 3)

items.title: Prevention of Noncommunicable Diseases
items.content: The WHO estimates that a significant proportion of noncommunicable diseases, including cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and cancers, can be prevented through lifestyle changes. Effective policy measures like tobacco taxes and smoking bans have proven to reduce tobacco use.
keyPoints: 80% of cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes cases, along with 40% of cancer cases, are preventable through healthier diets, increased physical activity, and avoiding tobacco.
Policy changes such as tobacco taxes and smoking bans effectively decrease tobacco consumption.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · “NONCOMMUNICABLE” CHRONIC DISEASES (part 3)

items.title: Global Prevalence and Impact of Mental Health Disorders
items.content: Mental health disorders are highly prevalent worldwide, affecting approximately 1 in every 8 people. Depression and anxiety disorders alone impact over 580 million individuals. Despite this high prevalence, a significant number of these disorders remain undiagnosed or untreated, contributing to mental health being the second leading cause of years lost to disability globally.
keyPoints: In 2019, ~970 million people worldwide lived with a mental health disorder.
More than 580 million people suffer from depression and anxiety disorders.
>700,000 people die by suicide annually.
Depressive disorders are the second leading cause of years lost to disability.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · MENTAL AND NEUROLOGIC HEALTH

items.title: Barriers to Mental Health Treatment in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
items.content: Low- and middle-income countries face significant challenges in addressing mental health disorders, primarily due to insufficient healthcare funding (<1% of health expenditures) and a lack of skilled personnel. This results in limited access to effective therapies for the majority of patients, particularly those living in poverty or rural areas.
keyPoints: Most low- and middle-income countries devote <1% of their health expenditures to mental health.
Sub-Saharan African countries have only a handful of psychiatrists, mostly practicing in urban areas.
Barriers prevent poor patients from adhering to prescribed treatment regimens.
A study in Kenya showed no therapeutic blood levels of anti-seizure medications among epilepsy clinic patients.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · MENTAL AND NEUROLOGIC HEALTH

items.title: Integration of Clinical Practice and Public Health for Global Health Equity
items.content: Global health equity requires the integration of clinical practice with public health strategies to effectively address both communicable and chronic diseases. Neither approach alone is sufficient, as evidenced by successes in managing complex infectious diseases like HIV/AIDS and tuberculosis when prevention and care are combined.
keyPoints: Integration of prevention and care is crucial for managing complex infectious diseases.
Global health equity involves addressing both communicable and chronic diseases.
Efforts must strengthen failing health systems and address personnel crises.
items.reference: Global Issues in Medicine · CONCLUSION: TOWARD GLOBAL HEALTH EQUITY

items.title: Role of Academic Medical Centers in Global Health Equity
items.content: Academic medical centers play a pivotal role in addressing global health equity by developing training programs and linking their capacities with public institutions. This collaboration can lead to significant progress in healthcare delivery for the poor, despite limited reach.
keyPoints: Training programs at university hospitals cover clinical problems and health systems strengthening.
Linking academic centers with public health institutions enhances healthcare delivery.
Scholarship in global health equity is evolving rapidly.

items.title: Challenges of Access to Essential Health Services
items.content: At least half of the world's population lacks reliable access to essential health services, leading to millions of preventable deaths annually. The majority of these deaths occur in Africa and poorer regions of Asia due to failures in healthcare delivery.
keyPoints: Half of the global population lacks reliable access to essential health services.
Millions of preventable deaths occur each year, primarily in Africa and parts of Asia.
Deaths result from vaccine-preventable illnesses, childbirth complications, untreated infectious diseases, malaria, and waterborne illnesses.
